meta.title: SpeedyCAT Practice Question Bank — scraped sample set
meta.description: MCAT-format practice questions collected by scraping openly/freely-licensed web sources. Built to match the PRD `PracticeQuestion` data model, plus a `passage`/`passageId` extension for passage-based items.
meta.disclaimer: Exploratory sample set. NOT from AAMC full-length practice tests. No AAMC-originated content is included. Every item carries traceable source metadata. Items whose license is proprietary/unclear are explicitly flagged with sourceLicense containing 'LICENSE-UNCERTAIN' and a note; do not ship those without clearing rights.

extensions.sourceUrl: Direct URL of the scraped page (traceability; PRD requires source attribution).
extensions.passage: Full passage text for passage-based items (CARS). Null/omitted for discrete questions.
extensions.passageId: Shared id grouping questions that hang off the same passage. Null/omitted for discretes.

schemaNotes.choices: Array of 4 objects {label: A|B|C|D, text}. correctAnswer is the matching label letter.

questions.stem: What is the definition of a Brønsted-Lowry acid?
questions.explanation: A Brønsted-Lowry acid is defined as a proton (H+) donor; the species that accepts the proton is the Brønsted-Lowry base. (Electron-pair donor/acceptor are the Lewis definitions.)
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. The course's odd-answer key page (16.9) did not render during scraping; this definitional answer is independently verified.

hints.prompt: Bronsted-Lowry acid-base theory classifies substances according to the transfer of which species?
hints.rationale: Bronsted-Lowry theory is defined entirely by the donation and acceptance of protons (H+), unlike the Lewis theory, which is based on electron pairs.

hints.prompt: Two of the four definitions offered in the question describe donating or accepting an electron pair. Those two definitions belong to which acid-base theory?
hints.rationale: Definitions based on donating or accepting electron pairs are the Lewis definitions, not the proton-based Bronsted-Lowry definitions.

hints.prompt: Using the previous step, which two of the question's options can be eliminated because they define Lewis acids/bases rather than a Bronsted-Lowry acid?
hints.rationale: The two electron-pair definitions are the Lewis definitions, so they can be ruled out, leaving the two proton-based options to decide between.

questions.stem: What is the conjugate base of HF?
questions.explanation: Removing a proton (H+) from HF leaves F-, which is its conjugate base.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim; answer independently verified (definitional).

hints.prompt: To form the conjugate base of an acid, what change is made to the acid's formula?
hints.rationale: A conjugate base is what remains after a Bronsted acid donates (loses) one proton, so you remove an H+.

hints.prompt: Which option describes forming a conjugate ACID (the opposite of what the question asks) from HF?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adding an H+ to HF to make H2F+

choices.label: D
choices.text: Adding a hydroxide ion to HF

hints.rationale: Adding a proton to HF yields the conjugate acid H2F+, which is the reverse of forming a conjugate base, so it is a trap.

hints.prompt: A conjugate base forms by losing a positively charged proton, so it cannot itself be a cation. Which two of the options are cations (positively charged) and can be ruled out?
hints.rationale: H2F+ and H3O+ each carry a positive charge, but removing a proton from neutral HF cannot produce a cation, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: What pH does an acidic solution have?

choices.label: D
choices.text: The definition of acidity depends on the molecule.

questions.explanation: At 25 °C pure (neutral) water has pH 7. Acidic solutions have [H3O+] greater than 1.0×10^-7 M, which corresponds to pH < 7.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim; answer independently verified (definitional).

hints.prompt: On the pH scale at 25 C, what pH value corresponds to a neutral solution such as pure water?
hints.rationale: At 25 C pure water is neutral, with equal H3O+ and OH-, giving a pH of 7, the midpoint of the scale.

hints.prompt: How are the hydronium concentration [H3O+] and pH related?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are inversely related: a higher [H3O+] gives a lower pH

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are directly related: a higher [H3O+] gives a higher pH

hints.rationale: Because pH = -log[H3O+], the negative logarithm makes pH decrease as [H3O+] increases.

hints.prompt: Two of the main question's options describe solutions that are NOT acidic. Which pair describes a neutral or basic solution and can be eliminated?

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'pH > 7' and 'the definition of acidity depends on the molecule'

hints.rationale: A pH of 7 is neutral and a pH above 7 is basic, so both describe non-acidic solutions and can be ruled out.

questions.stem: What is the pH of a solution with [H3O+] = 4.1 × 10^-5 M?
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 9 Acids and Bases
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim; answer independently verified by calculation.

hints.prompt: Which formula gives the pH of a solution from its hydronium ion concentration?
hints.rationale: pH is defined as the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydronium ion concentration.

hints.prompt: Since 4.1 is between 1 and 10, roughly what is log(4.1)? (Recall log 1 = 0 and log 10 = 1.)
hints.rationale: log(4.1) is about 0.61, since 4.1 lies between 1 (log = 0) and 10 (log = 1).

hints.prompt: For [H3O+] = 4.1 x 10^-5 M, two of the answer choices are clearly too high to be the pH. Which pair can be eliminated as too high?
hints.rationale: Because 4.1 x 10^-5 M is more concentrated than 1 x 10^-5 M (pH 5), the pH must be below 5, so 5.00 and the basic value 9.61 are too high.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a nonpolar (hydrophobic) amino acid?
questions.explanation: Valine has a branched hydrocarbon (isopropyl) side chain and is nonpolar/hydrophobic. Serine (–OH) and tyrosine (phenol) are polar, and lysine is positively charged.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 14 Proteins
questions.notes: correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.14: Q1 = c).

hints.prompt: What structural feature makes an amino acid side chain nonpolar (hydrophobic)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is made mostly of hydrocarbon (C-H) groups with no charged or polar groups

choices.label: C
choices.text: It carries a full positive or negative charge

choices.label: D
choices.text: It forms many hydrogen bonds with water

hints.rationale: Nonpolar side chains are built from hydrocarbon groups that lack polar or charged atoms, so they do not interact favorably with water.

hints.prompt: Which listed side-chain feature signals a POLAR amino acid rather than a hydrophobic one?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A hydroxyl (-OH) group, as found in serine

choices.label: D
choices.text: A side chain containing only C-H bonds

hints.rationale: A hydroxyl group is polar and hydrogen-bonds with water, marking a side chain (such as serine's) as polar rather than hydrophobic.

hints.prompt: Two of the amino acids listed carry a hydroxyl (-OH) group, which makes them polar. Which pair can be eliminated for that reason?
hints.rationale: Serine's side chain has an -OH and tyrosine's phenol ring bears an -OH, so both are polar and can be ruled out.

questions.stem: Which of the following elements is not found in amino acids?
questions.explanation: The standard amino acids are built from carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen (with sulfur in cysteine and methionine). Phosphorus is not part of the amino acid structure itself.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 14 Proteins
questions.notes: correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.14: Q2 = b).

hints.prompt: Which two functional groups are present in every amino acid and give the molecule its name?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A phosphate group and a sulfate group

choices.label: B
choices.text: An amino group and a carboxyl group

choices.label: C
choices.text: A hydroxyl group and a phosphate group

hints.rationale: Every amino acid contains an amino group and a carboxylic acid (carboxyl) group, which is the origin of the name amino acid.

hints.prompt: The amino group (-NH2) found in every amino acid is composed of which elements?
hints.rationale: An amino group (-NH2) consists of a nitrogen atom bonded to hydrogen atoms.

hints.prompt: The question asks which element is NOT present, so any element clearly found in the amino group can be eliminated as the answer. Which two options are present in every amino acid's amino group and can be ruled out?
hints.rationale: The amino group (-NH2) contains both nitrogen and hydrogen, so neither of those can be the element that is absent.

questions.stem: Hydrogen bonding is most important in stabilizing the ________ structure of many proteins.
questions.explanation: Secondary structure (α-helices and β-pleated sheets) is held together mainly by hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl (C=O) and amide (N–H) groups.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 14 Proteins
questions.notes: correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.14: Q13 = b).

hints.prompt: In proteins, which type of bond joins amino acids together to form the PRIMARY structure (the sequence itself)?
hints.rationale: Primary structure is the chain of amino acids linked by covalent peptide bonds, not by hydrogen bonds.

hints.prompt: Tertiary and quaternary structure are stabilized mainly by side-chain (R-group) interactions. The hydrogen bonds in this question, however, form between which parts of the protein?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The polypeptide backbone (main-chain C=O and N-H groups)

hints.rationale: The hydrogen bonds described are backbone-to-backbone (C=O with H-N), which distinguishes them from the side-chain interactions that stabilize higher-order structure.

hints.prompt: Primary structure is held together by covalent peptide bonds, and quaternary structure requires more than one polypeptide chain. Which two levels can be eliminated for backbone hydrogen bonding within a single chain's regular folds?
hints.rationale: Primary structure relies on covalent peptide bonds (not hydrogen bonds) and quaternary structure involves multiple chains, so both are ruled out.

questions.stem: The ________ is the basic unit of life.
questions.explanation: Cell theory holds that the cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e (hosted on Biology LibreTexts), 2.1 Cell Structure — Review Questions
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the official answer key publicly; this answer is independently verified from the same CC BY source text.

hints.prompt: In the hierarchy of biological organization, which sequence runs from the SIMPLEST to the most complex level?
hints.rationale: Biological organization ascends from cells to tissues to organs to organisms, each level built from the previous one.

hints.prompt: Which statement about tissues, organs, and whole organisms is TRUE and shows none of them is the most basic unit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each of them is composed of many smaller living building blocks

choices.label: B
choices.text: None of them can be divided into anything smaller

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are each a single, indivisible unit

hints.rationale: Tissues, organs, and organisms are all built from many smaller units, so none of them is the most basic (indivisible) unit of life.

hints.prompt: Two of the options are the LARGEST, most complex levels of organization, so they cannot be the most basic unit. Which pair is the least basic (most complex)?
hints.rationale: A whole organism and an organ are high-level structures built from many simpler parts, so neither is the most basic unit.

questions.stem: Which of the following organisms is a prokaryote?
questions.explanation: E. coli is a bacterium and therefore a prokaryote. Amoeba and charophyte algae are eukaryotes, and a virus is not a cellular organism.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e (hosted on Biology LibreTexts), 2.1 Cell Structure — Review Questions
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the official answer key publicly; answer independently verified from the same CC BY source text.

hints.prompt: Prokaryotes are organisms whose cells lack a membrane-bound nucleus. Which group of living things consists of prokaryotes?
hints.rationale: Bacteria are prokaryotes (they have no membrane-bound nucleus), whereas animals, plants, and fungi are eukaryotes.

hints.prompt: Amoeba and charophyte algae are eukaryotes, whose cells each contain a membrane-bound nucleus. Based on the definition of a prokaryote, can either one be a prokaryote?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because all single-celled organisms are prokaryotes

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because having a true nucleus makes them eukaryotic, not prokaryotic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because algae are always prokaryotes

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the amoeba can be a prokaryote

hints.rationale: A membrane-bound nucleus is the hallmark of eukaryotes, so amoeba and charophyte algae cannot be prokaryotes.

hints.prompt: After setting the eukaryotes aside, the 'influenza A virus' option can look tempting. Why is a virus NOT a prokaryote?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A virus is a specialized type of bacterium

choices.label: B
choices.text: A virus has a membrane-bound nucleus

choices.label: C
choices.text: A virus is a large multicellular organism

choices.label: D
choices.text: A virus is not a cell at all, so it is neither prokaryotic nor eukaryotic

hints.rationale: Prokaryote and eukaryote are classifications of cells, and a virus is acellular, so it cannot be a prokaryote.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not a component of the endomembrane system?
questions.explanation: The endomembrane system includes the nuclear envelope, ER, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, vesicles, and plasma membrane. Mitochondria are not part of it (they have their own double membrane and likely arose endosymbiotically).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e (hosted on Biology LibreTexts), 2.1 Cell Structure — Review Questions
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the official answer key publicly; answer independently verified from the same CC BY source text.

hints.prompt: What is the endomembrane system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The group of membranes and organelles that work together to modify, package, and transport proteins and lipids

choices.label: B
choices.text: The set of all organelles that produce ATP

choices.label: C
choices.text: The network of protein fibers that give the cell its shape

choices.label: D
choices.text: The collection of free ribosomes floating in the cytoplasm

hints.rationale: The endomembrane system is a set of interacting membranes and organelles that process and transport proteins and lipids through the cell.

hints.prompt: The endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus are directly linked by transport vesicles in the protein-processing pathway. What does this shared pathway tell you about them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both are outside the endomembrane system

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both are part of the endomembrane system

hints.rationale: The ER and Golgi are connected stations in the vesicle-trafficking pathway, so both belong to the endomembrane system.

hints.prompt: A student might pick 'lysosome,' thinking its digestive role sets it apart. Why is the lysosome actually NOT the exception here?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Lysosomes form from the Golgi and belong to the membrane-trafficking pathway

hints.rationale: Lysosomes bud from the Golgi within the vesicle-trafficking pathway, so they are part of the endomembrane system and not the exception.

questions.stem: Which of the following is most likely to have the greatest concentration of smooth endoplasmic reticulum?

choices.label: C
choices.text: a cell that makes steroid hormones

choices.label: D
choices.text: a cell that engages in photosynthesis

questions.explanation: Smooth ER synthesizes lipids, including steroids, so steroid-hormone–producing cells (e.g., in the gonads or adrenal cortex) are especially rich in smooth ER. Enzyme (protein) secretion relies on rough ER.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e (hosted on Biology LibreTexts), 2.1 Cell Structure — Review Questions
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the official answer key publicly; answer independently verified from the same CC BY source text.

hints.prompt: What is the main function of SMOOTH endoplasmic reticulum, as opposed to rough ER?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Synthesis of lipids (including steroids) and detoxification

choices.label: C
choices.text: Production of most of the cell's ATP

hints.rationale: Smooth ER lacks ribosomes and specializes in lipid and steroid synthesis and in detoxification, while rough ER makes proteins.

hints.prompt: The option 'a cell that secretes enzymes' is a classic trap. Because enzymes are proteins, which organelle does an enzyme-secreting cell rely on most?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rough ER (studded with ribosomes for protein synthesis)

hints.rationale: Enzymes are proteins built on the ribosomes of rough ER, so an enzyme-secreting cell is rich in rough (not smooth) ER, making it a trap here.

hints.prompt: Which two options describe cells whose main jobs depend on machinery OTHER than smooth ER (protein synthesis or chloroplasts) and so can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'a cell that secretes enzymes' and 'a cell that engages in photosynthesis'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'a cell that makes steroid hormones' and 'a cell that destroys pathogens'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'a cell that secretes enzymes' and 'a cell that makes steroid hormones'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'a cell that destroys pathogens' and 'a cell that engages in photosynthesis'

hints.rationale: Enzyme secretion depends on rough ER (ribosomes) and photosynthesis depends on chloroplasts, so neither relies mainly on smooth ER.

questions.stem: To which of the following classes of sugars does glucose belong?
questions.explanation: Glucose is a six-carbon sugar, so it is a hexose.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 12 Carbohydrates
questions.notes: correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.12: Q7 = b).

hints.prompt: Monosaccharides are named by their number of carbon atoms. What do the prefixes tetr-, pent-, hex-, and hept- indicate?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of carbon atoms in the sugar

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of rings in the sugar

hints.rationale: Prefixes such as tetr- (4), pent- (5), hex- (6), and hept- (7) state how many carbon atoms a monosaccharide has.

hints.prompt: Classifying a sugar as a tetrose, pentose, hexose, or heptose depends only on counting its carbons. To classify glucose this way, what must you determine about glucose?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of carbon atoms it contains

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether it is found in fruit

hints.rationale: Classifying a sugar as tetrose/pentose/hexose/heptose requires counting its carbon atoms, nothing else.

hints.prompt: Once you count glucose's carbons, two options with the most different carbon counts (four and seven) can be eliminated. Which pair is that?
hints.rationale: A tetrose has four carbons and a heptose has seven, the counts most different from glucose's, so both can be ruled out.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a disaccharide?
questions.explanation: Lactose is a disaccharide of glucose and galactose. Galactose and fructose are monosaccharides, and amylose is a polysaccharide (a component of starch).
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 12 Carbohydrates
questions.notes: correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.12: Q8 = b).

hints.prompt: How is a disaccharide defined?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A sugar made of two monosaccharide units joined together

choices.label: C
choices.text: A polymer of many hundreds of sugar units

choices.label: D
choices.text: A sugar that contains no carbon

hints.rationale: A disaccharide consists of exactly two monosaccharide units linked by a glycosidic bond.

hints.prompt: Two of the options, galactose and fructose, are each a single sugar unit. What class of carbohydrate is a single sugar unit?
hints.rationale: A single sugar unit is a monosaccharide, so galactose and fructose cannot be disaccharides.

hints.prompt: The option 'amylose' can be tempting. How many sugar units make up amylose, and what does that make it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exactly two units, making it a disaccharide

choices.label: B
choices.text: One unit, making it a monosaccharide

choices.label: C
choices.text: Many hundreds of units, making it a polysaccharide (a component of starch)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zero units, so it is not a carbohydrate

hints.rationale: Amylose is a long polymer of many glucose units (a polysaccharide and a component of starch), so it has far more than two units.

questions.stem: A carbohydrate with a C=O bond at carbon 2 would be classified as a(n)
questions.explanation: A carbonyl (C=O) on an internal carbon such as C2 is a ketone group, making the sugar a ketose. An aldose has its carbonyl at C1 (an aldehyde). Triose/hexose describe carbon count, not carbonyl position.
questions.sourceName: LibreTexts — General, Organic, and Biochemistry with Problems, Case Studies, and Activities (Roosevelt University), Ch. 12 Carbohydrates
questions.notes: correctAnswer from the course's Chapter 16 answer key (16.12: Q3 = b).

hints.prompt: In organic chemistry, a carbonyl (C=O) group on a terminal (end) carbon is an aldehyde. What is a carbonyl group on an INTERNAL carbon called?
hints.rationale: A carbonyl located on an internal (non-terminal) carbon is a ketone, whereas a terminal carbonyl is an aldehyde.

hints.prompt: The carbohydrate in the question has its C=O at carbon 2, an internal carbon. Which functional group is therefore present?
hints.rationale: A carbonyl on the internal carbon C2 (not the terminal C1) is a ketone group.

hints.prompt: Two of the answer choices (triose, hexose) describe the sugar's number of CARBONS, not the position of its carbonyl group. Since the question is about carbonyl position, which pair is off-topic and can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Triose (three carbons) and hexose (six carbons) classify a sugar by carbon count, which does not describe carbonyl position, so both are off-topic here.

questions.stem: In the scientific method, a hypothesis is a(n) ________.
questions.explanation: A hypothesis is a tentative, testable proposed explanation for an observed phenomenon, generated before data are collected.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e, Ch. 1 Review Questions
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solution Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 1 Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: Which sequence correctly orders the early steps of the scientific method?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Test with an experiment -> make an observation -> form a hypothesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Make an observation -> form a hypothesis -> test it with an experiment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Form a hypothesis -> make an observation -> never test it

choices.label: D
choices.text: Take a measurement -> publish -> then observe

hints.rationale: The scientific method typically moves from making an observation, to forming a hypothesis, to testing it experimentally.

hints.prompt: The option 'test' refers to the experiment that evaluates a hypothesis. Is a hypothesis the same thing as the test itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, the hypothesis is the idea being evaluated, while the test is the procedure that evaluates it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, a hypothesis and a test are identical

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, the hypothesis is the act performed in the lab

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, the hypothesis is the raw data that is collected

hints.rationale: A hypothesis is the proposed idea under investigation, not the experimental test used to check it, so the two are distinct.

hints.prompt: An observation and a measurement are both pieces of information COLLECTED from the world. Since a hypothesis is formulated before data confirm it, which two options can be eliminated as collected data rather than a proposed idea?
hints.rationale: Observations and measurements are empirical data gathered from the world, whereas a hypothesis is an idea proposed beforehand, so those two are ruled out.

questions.stem: ________ is most well-known for proposing his hierarchy of needs.
questions.explanation: Abraham Maslow proposed the hierarchy of needs, a humanistic motivation theory ascending from physiological needs to self-actualization.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e, Ch. 1 Review Questions
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solution Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 1 Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: To which broad school of psychology does the hierarchy of needs belong?
hints.rationale: The hierarchy of needs is a humanistic theory of motivation that emphasizes personal growth toward self-actualization.

hints.prompt: The hierarchy of needs is often drawn as a pyramid that ascends from physiological needs. Where are physiological needs located in it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At the base, as the most fundamental needs

choices.label: B
choices.text: At the very top, as the final goal

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are not considered needs at all

hints.rationale: The hierarchy ascends from physiological needs upward, so those needs form the base of the pyramid.

hints.prompt: To identify the theorist, look for the one whose work centers on motivation and self-actualization. Which research theme should the correct person be associated with?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Motivation and the drive toward self-actualization

choices.label: C
choices.text: The rules of grammar and language

hints.rationale: The hierarchy of needs is a theory of motivation culminating in self-actualization, so its author is identified with that theme.

questions.stem: The operant conditioning chamber (aka ________ box) is a device used to study the principles of operant conditioning.
questions.explanation: The operant conditioning chamber is commonly called the 'Skinner box,' after B. F. Skinner, who used it to study how reinforcement and punishment shape behavior.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e, Ch. 1 Review Questions
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solution Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 1 Q7 = A.

hints.prompt: What does operant conditioning study?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How reinforcement and punishment shape behavior

choices.label: B
choices.text: How traits are inherited through genes

hints.rationale: Operant conditioning examines how consequences (reinforcement and punishment) increase or decrease behaviors.

hints.prompt: The operant conditioning chamber is a device built specifically to study operant conditioning. Its namesake is therefore a psychologist tied to which area of research?
hints.rationale: Because the chamber is used to study operant conditioning, it is named for a psychologist associated with reinforcement and punishment.

hints.prompt: Three of the four names are not primarily linked to operant conditioning. To pick the right one, which research focus should you look for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Studying how consequences (reinforcement and punishment) change voluntary behavior

choices.label: B
choices.text: Measuring the wavelengths of visible light

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mapping the grammar rules of languages

hints.rationale: The correct namesake is defined by operant conditioning research, shaping voluntary behavior through reinforcement and punishment.

questions.stem: An individual's consistent pattern of thought and behavior is known as a(n) ________.
questions.explanation: Personality is defined as an individual's consistent, characteristic pattern of thinking, feeling, and behaving.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e, Ch. 1 Review Questions
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solution Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 1 Q9 = C.

hints.prompt: The phrase 'a consistent pattern of thought and behavior' emphasizes what about a person?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A stable, characteristic way of thinking and behaving over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: A single momentary reaction to one event

choices.label: D
choices.text: An inherited physical trait such as eye color

hints.rationale: The key idea is a stable, characteristic pattern that persists over time, not a one-time reaction.

hints.prompt: Does 'a consistent pattern of thought and behavior' describe a single, narrow mental process, or a broad characteristic of the whole person?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A broad, overall characteristic of the whole person

hints.rationale: A consistent pattern spanning both thought and behavior is a broad, person-level characteristic rather than one isolated process.

hints.prompt: The correct term must name a person's whole, enduring pattern, not a single developmental stage, a single ability, or a single sensory process. Which criterion should the answer meet?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It names one narrow developmental milestone

choices.label: B
choices.text: It names one specific sensory process

choices.label: C
choices.text: It names a person's overall, enduring pattern of thinking and behaving

choices.label: D
choices.text: It names a single automatic reflex

hints.rationale: The answer must be the broad, whole-person construct (an enduring pattern of thought and behavior), so narrow or specific terms are ruled out.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes sociology as a subject?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The study of society and social interaction

questions.explanation: Sociology is the systematic study of society and social interaction—how groups, institutions, and social forces shape human behavior.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 1 Section Quiz
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 1 Q1 = C.

hints.prompt: The explanation stresses that sociology studies groups, institutions, and social forces. Given that, does sociology focus on the individual or on the group/collective level?
hints.rationale: Sociology studies groups, institutions, and social forces, so its focus is the collective or social level rather than the isolated individual.

hints.prompt: Because sociology works at the social/group level, the option 'The study of individual behavior' is a mismatch. What level does that option actually target?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The single-individual level, which is too narrow for sociology

choices.label: B
choices.text: The societal level, which matches sociology

choices.label: D
choices.text: The level of planets and galaxies

hints.rationale: The study of individual behavior targets one person, not the group or societal level that defines sociology, so it is a mismatch.

hints.prompt: Two options are not sociology's central definition: one targets a single person's behavior and one targets money, goods, and markets. Which pair can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'The study of society and social interaction' and 'The study of cultures'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The study of individual behavior' and 'The study of society and social interaction'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'The study of individual behavior' and 'The study of economics'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'The study of cultures' and 'The study of economics'

hints.rationale: One option focuses on the individual and another on money and markets (economics), neither of which is sociology's core focus on society and social interaction.

questions.stem: Who believed that the history of society was one of class struggle?
questions.explanation: Karl Marx argued that society's history is fundamentally a history of class struggle between those who own the means of production and those who labor for them.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 1 Section Quiz
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 1 Q9 = B.

hints.prompt: The idea that 'the history of society is one of class struggle' centers on conflict between which groups?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Those who own the means of production and those who labor for them

hints.rationale: Class struggle refers to conflict between the owners of the means of production and the workers who labor for them.

hints.prompt: The class-struggle view frames the history of society mainly in terms of what?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Individual dreams and the unconscious mind

hints.rationale: The class-struggle perspective sees society as driven by economic conflict between social classes.

hints.prompt: To identify the correct thinker, look for the one whose theory centers on conflict between economic classes. Which focus identifies that thinker?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Viewing society as a set of harmonious, self-regulating parts

choices.label: B
choices.text: Viewing society as ongoing conflict between owners and workers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treating society as a stage where people perform roles

hints.rationale: The class-struggle thinker is defined by seeing society as economic conflict between owners and laborers, unlike the other perspectives.

questions.stem: A symbolic interactionist may compare social interactions to:
questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism (via Goffman's dramaturgical analysis) compares social interaction to a theatrical performance in which people play roles on a social 'stage.'
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 1 Section Quiz
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 1 Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: Sociological perspectives are often summarized by a central metaphor. What is such a guiding metaphor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A comparison that represents how a theory views social life

choices.label: C
choices.text: A laboratory chemical used in tests

hints.rationale: A guiding metaphor is an analogy a theory uses to represent social life and convey its core viewpoint.

hints.prompt: This perspective is called 'dramaturgical.' The root of that word (drama) signals a metaphor drawn from which domain?
hints.rationale: Dramaturgical comes from drama, pointing to a metaphor based on the theater and the stage.

hints.prompt: A performance-based metaphor is about people acting out parts in front of others. Which two options are LEAST like a performance metaphor and can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Comparing social interaction to bodily organs or to conflicts does not fit a performance/role metaphor, so both can be set aside.
